knowledge.what_it_is: Business Intelligence (BI) is an umbrella term that encompasses various tools, technologies, and methodologies used to analyze and visualize data to support business decision-making. As a BI Analyst, you will work with stakeholders to identify business needs, design and develop reports, dashboards, and other visualizations to help them make informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Data warehousing — designing and implementing a centralized repository for storing and managing data from various sources
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes — understanding how to extract data from multiple sources, transform it into a standardized format, and load it into a target system
OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) cubes — creating multidimensional databases that enable fast and efficient analysis of large datasets
Data visualization tools — learning popular libraries such as Tableau, Power BI, or QlikView to create interactive dashboards and reports
SQL fundamentals — understanding how to write effective queries using SQL dialects such as T-SQL, PL/SQL, or MySQL
Data modeling — creating conceptual, logical, and physical data models to represent business entities and relationships
BI platforms — familiarizing yourself with popular platforms like Microsoft Power BI, Tableau, or SAP BusinessObjects

knowledge.what_it_is: Business Intelligence (BI) is the process of analyzing and interpreting data to inform business decisions, using tools like SQL, Excel, Tableau, Power BI, and QlikView. As a BI Analyst, you will be responsible for extracting insights from large datasets to drive strategic planning, improve operational efficiency, and optimize resource allocation.
what_you_will_learn: Data-driven decision making — understanding the importance of data in informing business decisions
BI tools and technologies — familiarizing yourself with popular BI platforms like Tableau, Power BI, QlikView, and Google Data Studio
Data visualization best practices — learning how to effectively communicate insights through charts, graphs, and dashboards
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and metrics — identifying relevant metrics for business success and tracking progress over time
BI lifecycle management — understanding the stages of BI project development, from requirements gathering to deployment and maintenance
Data governance and security — ensuring data quality, integrity, and compliance with regulatory standards
Collaboration and stakeholder engagement — working with stakeholders to understand their needs and develop effective solutions

knowledge.what_it_is: A BI Analyst is responsible for extracting insights from data to inform business decisions, using tools like SQL, Excel, and visualization software. Unlike Data Scientists, who focus on advanced statistical modeling and machine learning, BI Analysts prioritize data storytelling and communication.
what_you_will_learn: Data Modeling — creating conceptual models of business processes and data flows to guide analysis
Dimensional Modeling — designing star and snowflake schemas for efficient querying and aggregation
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Process — understanding how to extract data from various sources, transform it into a usable format, and load it into a database or data warehouse
SQL Query Optimization — writing efficient SQL queries using indexes, joins, subqueries, and aggregations
Data Visualization Best Practices — creating effective dashboards and reports using tools like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js
Business Intelligence Tools — understanding the strengths and limitations of popular BI platforms like Microsoft Power BI, Tableau, or QlikView
Data Governance — implementing policies and procedures for data security, access control, and quality assurance
Stakeholder Management — communicating insights and recommendations to non-technical stakeholders using clear language and visualizations

knowledge.what_it_is: A BI Analyst's skills encompass data analysis, visualization, and business acumen to extract insights from complex datasets and communicate findings effectively to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Data modeling — creating conceptual and logical models of data structures using tools like ERwin or Lucidchart
SQL querying — writing efficient queries in SQL to retrieve and manipulate data, including joins, subqueries, and aggregations
Data visualization best practices — designing effective dashboards and reports using tools like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js
Business intelligence tool proficiency — mastering the capabilities of specific BI platforms such as Microsoft Power BI, Tableau, or QlikView
Data storytelling — crafting compelling narratives to convey insights and recommendations to stakeholders
Communication skills — effectively presenting complex data insights to non-technical stakeholders using clear language and visual aids
Analytical thinking — applying statistical techniques and data analysis methods to identify trends, patterns, and correlations in data

knowledge.what_it_is: As a BI Analyst, your primary responsibility is to extract insights from data to inform business decisions. This involves working closely with stakeholders to understand their needs and developing reports, dashboards, and visualizations that meet those requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Data requirement gathering — identifying key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics for reporting
Business stakeholder communication — effectively conveying complex data insights to non-technical stakeholders
Report development — creating interactive reports using tools like Tableau, Power BI, or QlikView
Dashboard creation — designing intuitive dashboards that provide at-a-glance visibility into key business metrics
Data visualization best practices — applying principles of visual design to communicate complex data insights effectively
BI tool configuration — setting up and configuring BI tools such as SQL Server Reporting Services (SSRS), Oracle Business Intelligence (OBI), or SAP BusinessObjects
Data security and governance — ensuring that sensitive data is properly secured and governed in accordance with organizational policies
Collaboration with IT — working closely with IT teams to ensure smooth integration of BI solutions with existing infrastructure

knowledge.what_it_is: Key metrics are quantifiable indicators used to measure and evaluate business performance, progress, or success. In the context of Business Intelligence (BI), key metrics help stakeholders make informed decisions by providing a clear understanding of what is happening within an organization.
what_you_will_learn: Gross Margin Ratio — calculating revenue minus cost of goods sold divided by revenue, to evaluate profitability
Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) — measuring the average cost of acquiring one customer, including marketing and sales expenses
Return on Investment (ROI) — evaluating the return generated by an investment or project compared to its costs
Net Promoter Score (NPS) — gauging customer satisfaction and loyalty through a single metric
Average Order Value (AOV) — analyzing revenue per transaction, influencing pricing strategies and promotions
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — estimating the total value of a customer over their lifetime, informing retention efforts
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — defining and tracking specific metrics to measure progress toward organizational goals

knowledge.what_it_is: Stakeholder identification is a crucial step in Business Intelligence (BI) analysis, where you identify and categorize individuals or groups who have an interest or impact on business decisions. This process helps BI analysts understand the needs of stakeholders, prioritize their requirements, and develop effective solutions.
what_you_will_learn: Stakeholder categories — identifying primary, secondary, and tertiary stakeholders based on their level of influence and interest
Stakeholder mapping — creating a visual representation of stakeholder relationships using tools like MindMeister or Lucidchart
SWOT analysis — analyzing the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats associated with each stakeholder group
RAG (Red-Amber-Green) rating — assigning a color-coded rating to stakeholders based on their level of engagement and satisfaction
Stakeholder personas — creating detailed profiles of key stakeholders, including their goals, motivations, and pain points
BI tool integration — using tools like Tableau or Power BI to integrate stakeholder data and create interactive dashboards
Data visualization best practices — applying principles of effective data visualization to communicate stakeholder insights
Stakeholder engagement strategies — developing plans to engage with stakeholders, including regular meetings, surveys, and feedback mechanisms

knowledge.what_it_is: Business Intelligence (BI) operations refer to the processes and activities involved in collecting, analyzing, and presenting data to support business decision-making. As a BI Analyst, you will work with various types of BI operations to extract insights from data.
what_you_will_learn: Data Extraction — extracting data from various sources using ETL tools like Informatica PowerCenter, Talend, or Apache NiFi
Data Transformation — converting raw data into a format suitable for analysis using SQL, pandas, or NumPy
Data Loading — loading transformed data into a data warehouse or database management system like Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server, or PostgreSQL
Data Merging — combining data from multiple sources using joins, unions, or set operations in SQL or Python
Data Filtering — applying filters to reduce data volume and improve query performance using SQL or pandas
Data Aggregation — grouping data by categories and calculating summary statistics like mean, median, or count using SQL or pandas
Data Visualization — presenting data insights using visualization tools like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js
Data Storytelling — communicating complex data insights to stakeholders through narratives and visualizations

knowledge.what_it_is: Operational Business Intelligence (BI) focuses on real-time data analysis and reporting to support business operations, leveraging tools like Tableau, Power BI, or QlikView. As a BI Analyst, you'll work with stakeholders to design and implement dashboards that drive informed decision-making.
what_you_will_learn: Data Warehousing — designing and implementing data warehouses using ETL tools like Informatica, Talend, or AWS Glue
ETL Process Design — creating efficient ETL processes for data integration, transformation, and loading into the data warehouse
Dimensional Modeling — applying Kimball's dimensional modeling approach to create a star schema for fast query performance
OLAP Cubes — building OLAP cubes using tools like Microsoft Analysis Services or Oracle OLAP
Data Visualization Best Practices — designing effective dashboards with Tableau, Power BI, or QlikView, including chart types and layout considerations
BI Tool Configuration — configuring and customizing BI tools for optimal performance, security, and user experience
Data Security and Governance — implementing data access controls, data masking, and auditing to ensure data integrity and compliance
Business Process Automation — integrating BI with business processes using workflow automation tools like Apache Airflow or Zapier

knowledge.what_it_is: Financial analysis and reporting is a critical function of Business Intelligence (BI) that involves extracting, transforming, and loading financial data from various sources to support business decision-making. As a BI Analyst, you will work with stakeholders to identify key performance indicators (KPIs), develop dashboards, and create reports to visualize financial data.
what_you_will_learn: Financial statement analysis — understanding the structure of balance sheets, income statements, and cash flow statements
Ratio analysis — calculating liquidity, profitability, and efficiency ratios using Excel formulas or SQL queries
Cash flow management — identifying cash inflows and outflows, forecasting future cash needs, and optimizing working capital
Budgeting and forecasting — developing financial models to predict revenue and expenses, creating rolling forecasts, and analyzing variances
Financial reporting — designing reports to meet regulatory requirements (e.g., GAAP, IFRS), creating ad-hoc reports for stakeholders, and ensuring data accuracy and integrity
Data visualization for finance — using tools like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js to create interactive dashboards and visualizations of financial data
Financial modeling — building models in Excel (e.g., NPV, IRR) or Python libraries (e.g., pandas, NumPy) to analyze investment opportunities and make informed decisions

knowledge.what_it_is: Marketing analytics involves using data and statistical methods to measure, analyze, and optimize marketing campaigns and strategies. As a BI Analyst, you will use data visualization tools and techniques to communicate insights and recommendations to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Google Analytics — understanding how to set up and track website interactions, including events, sessions, and goals
Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) — using statistical models to estimate the impact of marketing activities on business outcomes, such as revenue or sales
Customer Journey Mapping — creating visual representations of customer interactions across multiple touchpoints and channels
Segmentation Analysis — dividing customers into groups based on demographic, behavioral, or firmographic characteristics to target specific segments with tailored marketing strategies
A/B Testing (Split-Testing) — designing and executing experiments to compare the effectiveness of different marketing campaigns or messaging
Marketing Attribution Modeling — assigning credit to specific marketing channels for driving business outcomes, such as revenue or sales
Predictive Analytics — using statistical models and machine learning algorithms to forecast future marketing performance based on historical data
Data-Driven Marketing Automation (DDMA) — using data and analytics to automate and personalize marketing campaigns across multiple channels

knowledge.what_it_is: Operations in Business Intelligence (BI) refers to the management of data flows, workflows, and processes that enable business users to make informed decisions. It involves ensuring data quality, integrity, and security while optimizing data processing and delivery.
what_you_will_learn: ETL Tools — Extract, Transform, Load: using tools like Informatica PowerCenter, Talend, or Microsoft SSIS for data integration
Data Warehousing — designing and implementing a centralized repository for storing and managing business data with tools like Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, or Snowflake
Data Marts — creating smaller, subject-oriented databases to support specific business needs using tools like Oracle OLAP or IBM InfoSphere Data Architect
Data Governance — establishing policies and procedures for data management, security, and compliance with frameworks like ISO 27001 or NIST Cybersecurity Framework
Data Quality Metrics — measuring and monitoring data accuracy, completeness, and consistency using metrics like data coverage, data freshness, or data quality scores
Data Lineage — tracking the origin, processing, and usage of data to ensure transparency and accountability with tools like Apache Atlas or Informatica DataLineage
Change Data Capture (CDC) — detecting and capturing changes made to source systems for real-time data integration using CDC tools like Debezium or Oracle GoldenGate
Data Synchronization — maintaining consistency between multiple data sources or targets using techniques like incremental updates, batch processing, or event-driven architecture

knowledge.what_it_is: Human Resources (HR) is a critical function in organizations that deals with the management of an organization's workforce, including recruitment, training, benefits, and employee relations. As a BI Analyst, you will work closely with HR teams to provide data-driven insights on workforce planning, talent acquisition, and employee engagement.
what_you_will_learn: HR Metrics — defining and tracking key performance indicators (KPIs) for HR functions such as time-to-hire, employee turnover rate, and training effectiveness
Workforce Planning — using data to forecast future staffing needs, identify skill gaps, and develop strategic plans for talent acquisition and development
Talent Acquisition Analytics — analyzing recruitment metrics, source of hire, and candidate experience to optimize hiring processes and improve diversity and inclusion
Employee Engagement Metrics — designing and implementing surveys to measure employee satisfaction, sentiment analysis, and action planning to improve retention and productivity
Compensation Analysis — using data to analyze pay equity, market competitiveness, and compensation trends to inform salary adjustments and benefits planning
Benefits Administration — streamlining benefits enrollment, tracking usage, and analyzing claims data to optimize benefit offerings and reduce costs
Succession Planning — identifying key roles, developing talent pipelines, and creating succession plans to ensure business continuity and minimize risk
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Metrics — measuring progress towards DEI goals, analyzing employee demographics, and identifying areas for improvement

knowledge.what_it_is: Tactical Business Intelligence (BI) refers to the application of data analysis and visualization techniques to support short-term decision-making, typically within a specific business function or department. It involves using data to inform tactical decisions that drive immediate results.
what_you_will_learn: Data Storytelling — crafting narratives around data insights to communicate findings to stakeholders
Tactical Dashboarding — designing and building dashboards to monitor key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics in real-time
Alert Systems — setting up automated alerts for anomalies, exceptions, or threshold breaches using tools like Tableau Server or Power BI Embedded
What-If Analysis — using data modeling and simulation techniques to explore the impact of different scenarios on business outcomes
Root Cause Analysis (RCA) — applying statistical methods to identify the underlying causes of problems or trends in operational data
Tactical Reporting — creating ad-hoc reports to support short-term planning, such as sales forecasting or inventory management
Data-Driven Decision Making — using data visualization and analysis to inform tactical decisions, rather than relying on intuition or anecdotal evidence

knowledge.what_it_is: Strategic Business Intelligence (BI) involves using data and analytics to inform business decisions, drive growth, and improve performance. As a BI Analyst, you will work with stakeholders to identify key performance indicators (KPIs), develop data visualizations, and create reports that help organizations make informed strategic decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — defining and tracking metrics that measure business success, such as revenue growth, customer acquisition costs, and return on investment
Data Visualization Best Practices — creating interactive dashboards, using color theory, and applying storytelling techniques to communicate insights effectively
Business Intelligence Tools — working with popular tools like Tableau, Power BI, or QlikView to connect to data sources, create visualizations, and share reports
Data Storytelling — crafting narratives that highlight trends, patterns, and correlations in the data, using examples from real-world business scenarios
Strategic Planning Process — understanding how to integrate data analysis into the strategic planning process, including goal-setting, resource allocation, and performance monitoring
Stakeholder Management — working with stakeholders to identify needs, develop solutions, and communicate insights effectively, using techniques like stakeholder mapping and communication plans

knowledge.what_it_is: Descriptive analysis is a type of data analysis that summarizes and describes the main characteristics of a dataset, such as measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and dispersion (range, variance). It provides an overview of what happened in the past, without making any predictions or recommendations.
what_you_will_learn: Measures of central tendency — calculating mean, median, mode to describe the middle value of a dataset
Measures of dispersion — calculating range, variance, standard deviation to describe the spread of data values
Data visualization techniques — using plots (histograms, box plots) and charts (bar charts, scatter plots) to communicate findings
Summary statistics — calculating count, sum, minimum, maximum, and quartiles to summarize dataset characteristics
Data profiling — identifying patterns, trends, and outliers in a dataset using summary statistics and data visualization
Descriptive statistical measures for categorical variables — calculating frequency, percentage, mode, and cross-tabulation
Interpretation of descriptive analysis results — understanding the implications of findings for business decisions

knowledge.what_it_is: Diagnostic analysis is a type of data analysis that identifies and diagnoses problems or issues within an organization's business processes, using statistical methods and data visualization techniques. As a BI Analyst, diagnostic analysis helps to identify root causes of problems, optimize business processes, and improve overall performance.
what_you_will_learn: Identifying key performance indicators (KPIs) — selecting relevant metrics to measure business outcomes
Data profiling — analyzing data distribution, outliers, and missing values to understand data quality
Anomaly detection — using statistical methods like Z-score, IQR, or regression analysis to identify unusual patterns
Root cause analysis (RCA) — applying techniques like fishbone diagrams, 5 Whys, or Pareto charts to identify underlying causes
Data visualization for diagnostic purposes — creating visualizations like scatter plots, heat maps, or treemaps to highlight trends and correlations
Using statistical process control (SPC) methods — monitoring data against control limits to detect deviations from normal behavior
subtopics: data visualization libraries (e.g. tableau, power bi)

knowledge.what_it_is: Predictive analysis involves using statistical models and machine learning algorithms to forecast future outcomes based on historical data, enabling businesses to make informed decisions and optimize operations.
what_you_will_learn: Linear regression — modeling continuous target variables with linear relationships between predictors
Decision trees — building tree-based models for classification and regression tasks, including CART and ID3 algorithms
Random forests — combining multiple decision trees to improve model accuracy and reduce overfitting
Support vector machines (SVMs) — using kernel methods to classify data in high-dimensional spaces
Gradient boosting — iteratively adding weak models to create strong predictive models
Time series decomposition — separating time series data into trend, seasonality, and residual components
ARIMA modeling — fitting autoregressive integrated moving average models for forecasting
Exponential smoothing — using weighted averages of past observations to forecast future values

knowledge.what_it_is: Prescriptive analysis involves using data and analytics to make recommendations or predictions that inform business decisions, often leveraging machine learning algorithms and statistical models.
what_you_will_learn: Linear Programming — mathematical optimization technique for solving complex problems with multiple variables and constraints
Goal Seek Analysis — iterative process of adjusting input values to achieve a specific output value in Excel or other tools
Decision Trees — visual representation of decision-making processes, often used in business intelligence applications
Scoring Models — statistical models that assign scores to individuals or entities based on their characteristics and behavior
Optimization Techniques — methods for finding the best solution among multiple alternatives, such as linear programming, dynamic programming, or genetic algorithms
What-if Analysis — scenario-based analysis using data and analytics to explore potential outcomes of different decisions
Sensitivity Analysis — examination of how changes in input values affect output results, often used to validate model assumptions

knowledge.what_it_is: In Business Intelligence (BI), discrete and continuous variables are two fundamental types of data that differ in their measurement scale, granularity, and analytical approach. Discrete variables represent countable values or categories, while continuous variables can take any value within a given range.
what_you_will_learn: Discrete vs Continuous Variables — understanding the key differences between these two variable types, including examples of each
Nominal vs Ordinal Variables — recognizing that nominal variables are labels without inherent order, whereas ordinal variables have an inherent order but no quantitative value
Interval Scales — identifying interval scales as those with equal intervals between consecutive values, but lacking a true zero point (e.g., temperature in Celsius)
Ratio Scales — distinguishing ratio scales as those with equal intervals and a true zero point (e.g., weight in kilograms)
Continuous vs Discrete Data Types in SQL — understanding how to handle continuous and discrete data types in database management systems, including data type conversions and storage considerations
Data Visualization for Continuous Variables — learning best practices for visualizing continuous variables, such as using histograms, box plots, or density plots
Discrete Variable Analysis with Pandas — applying pandas functions (e.g., `value_counts`, `groupby`) to analyze discrete variables in Python
Continuous Variable Modeling with scikit-learn — understanding how to use scikit-learn libraries (e.g., `LinearRegression`, `DecisionTreeRegressor`) for modeling continuous variables

knowledge.what_it_is: In Business Intelligence (BI) analysis, categorical and numerical variables are two fundamental types of data that require different handling approaches.
what_you_will_learn: Categorical Variables — discrete values representing categories or labels, often used for grouping and filtering (e.g., country, color, category)
Numerical Variables — continuous or discrete values representing quantities or measurements, often used for aggregation and analysis (e.g., sales, temperature, population)
Data Types in SQL — understanding how to declare categorical and numerical variables as data types in SQL databases (e.g., VARCHAR, INT, FLOAT)
Pivot Tables — using pivot tables to summarize and analyze numerical data while maintaining relationships with categorical variables
Grouping and Aggregation — applying grouping functions (e.g., SUM, AVG) to numerical variables while handling categorical variables as filters or dimensions
Data Visualization — selecting appropriate visualization types for categorical vs numerical data (e.g., bar charts, scatter plots, heatmaps)
Dimensional Modeling — designing dimensional models that accommodate both categorical and numerical variables in a star or snowflake schema
ETL Processes — handling data type conversions and transformations during ETL processes to ensure accurate loading of categorical and numerical data

knowledge.what_it_is: Descriptive statistics are used to summarize and describe data, providing insights into its central tendency, dispersion, and distribution. In the context of BI Analysis, descriptive statistics help analysts understand data patterns, identify trends, and make informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Mean — calculating the average value using sample or population data
Median — finding the middle value in a dataset, handling outliers with quantiles
Mode — identifying the most frequently occurring value(s) in a dataset, including multimodal distributions
Standard Deviation (SD) — measuring dispersion from the mean, considering population and sample variability
Variance — calculating the average of squared differences from the mean, used for SD calculation
Range — finding the difference between maximum and minimum values, sensitive to outliers
Interquartile Range (IQR) — calculating the difference between 75th and 25th percentiles, robust against outliers
Skewness — measuring asymmetry in a distribution, indicating left or right skew
Kurtosis — quantifying the 'tailed-ness' of a distribution, comparing to normal distribution
subtopics: density plots and kernel density estimation

knowledge.what_it_is: Central tendency measures the central or typical value of a dataset, providing an overview of the data distribution. It's essential for BI analysts to understand and apply central tendency metrics to effectively communicate insights to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Mean — the average value calculated by summing all values and dividing by the number of observations
Median — the middle value in a sorted dataset, used when data is skewed or contains outliers
Mode — the most frequently occurring value in a dataset, useful for categorical data
Weighted Mean — calculates mean values with varying weights to account for different importance levels
Truncated Mean — ignores extreme values (outliers) to reduce their impact on the average
Interquartile Mean (IQR) — robust measure of central tendency, less affected by outliers
Geometric Mean — used for growth rates or ratios, especially in finance and economics
Harmonic Mean — suitable for datasets with varying weights or frequencies
Variance and Standard Deviation — understanding the spread around the mean is crucial for BI analysts
subtopics: central tendency metrics for skewed data

knowledge.what_it_is: The mode is the most frequently occurring value in a dataset, used to describe central tendency and understand data distribution. In business intelligence (BI) analysis, the mode is often used in conjunction with other measures of central tendency like mean and median to get a comprehensive understanding of data.
what_you_will_learn: Calculating mode using Excel's MODE.SNGL function — returns the most frequently occurring value in a range
Using pandas' mode() function in Python for Data Analysis — returns the most frequently occurring value in a Series or DataFrame
Identifying multi-modal distributions and handling tied modes — understanding how to handle datasets with multiple modes or tied frequencies
Mode calculation using SQL queries — writing SQL code to find the most frequent value in a column
Interpreting mode values in the context of data distribution — understanding how mode relates to mean, median, and standard deviation
Using mode as a summary statistic for categorical variables — applying mode to understand the most common category or group

knowledge.what_it_is: The mean, also known as the arithmetic mean or average, is a measure of central tendency that calculates the sum of all values divided by the number of observations. It's widely used in business intelligence to summarize large datasets and provide insights into trends and patterns.
what_you_will_learn: Calculating mean using Excel formulas (AVERAGE function) — understanding how to use built-in functions for simple calculations
Mean calculation using SQL (AVG function) — learning how to apply aggregate functions in database queries
Weighted mean — understanding how to assign different weights to observations and calculate the weighted average
Truncated mean — learning about the concept of truncating extreme values before calculating the mean
Interquartile mean — understanding how to use this robust measure of central tendency for skewed distributions

knowledge.what_it_is: The median is a measure of central tendency that represents the middle value in an ordered dataset, dividing it into two equal halves.
what_you_will_learn: Calculating median using the formula: Median = (n+1)/2th observation, where n is the number of observations
Understanding the difference between population and sample median, and when to use each
Using Excel's MEDIAN function or Python's numpy.median() function to calculate median in a dataset
Handling missing values when calculating median, including using mean substitution or listwise deletion methods
Interpreting median as a measure of central tendency, including its advantages over mean and mode
Visualizing median using box plots, including understanding the relationship between median and IQR (interquartile range)
Using R's quantile() function to calculate different types of medians, such as trimmed mean or winsorized mean

knowledge.what_it_is: Range is a measure of dispersion that calculates the difference between the highest and lowest values in a dataset, often used to understand the spread or variability of data in Business Intelligence (BI) analysis.
what_you_will_learn: Calculating range using the formula: Range = Maximum value - Minimum value
Understanding the limitations of range as a measure of dispersion, including its sensitivity to outliers and skewness
Using range in combination with other measures of dispersion, such as variance or standard deviation, for a more comprehensive understanding of data spread
Applying range in different business contexts, such as financial analysis (e.g., stock prices), customer behavior, or product performance
Visualizing range using box plots, histograms, or scatter plots to communicate insights effectively
Identifying and addressing outliers that may impact the accuracy of range calculations
Using range to compare data distributions across different groups or categories

knowledge.what_it_is: Variance measures the spread of a dataset, indicating how much individual data points deviate from the mean value. It's a crucial metric in business intelligence (BI) to understand data distribution and make informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Population Variance — calculating variance for an entire population using the formula σ^2 = Σ(x_i - μ)^2 / N
Sample Variance — estimating variance from a sample dataset using the formula s^2 = Σ(x_i - x̄)^2 / (n-1)
Covariance Matrix — representing the variance-covariance relationship between multiple variables in a dataset
Standard Deviation — calculating the square root of variance to represent data spread in a more interpretable unit
Coefficient of Variation (CV) — normalizing standard deviation by mean value to compare variability across datasets
Variance Analysis (ANOVA) — using F-statistic and p-value to determine if variance differences between groups are statistically significant
subtopics: levene's test for equality of variances
bartlett's test for equality of variances

knowledge.what_it_is: Standard Deviation (STD) is a measure of dispersion that calculates the average distance between each data point and the mean, used to understand the spread or variability of a dataset. In Business Intelligence (BI), STD is crucial for identifying outliers, normalizing data, and making informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Mean calculation — understanding how to calculate the arithmetic mean using various formulas (e.g., sample mean, population mean)
Population vs Sample Standard Deviation — knowing when to use each type of STD, including scenarios where sample size is small or large
Variance calculation — learning how to calculate variance from standard deviation and vice versa
Z-score calculation — understanding how to calculate z-scores using STD and mean for data normalization
Interpretation of results — knowing how to interpret STD values in the context of a dataset, including identifying outliers and areas of high variability

knowledge.what_it_is: Interquartile Range (IQR) is a measure of dispersion that calculates the difference between the 75th percentile (Q3) and the 25th percentile (Q1) of a dataset, used to identify outliers and understand data distribution in Business Intelligence (BI) analysis.
what_you_will_learn: Calculating IQR using Excel's QUARTILE function or Python's numpy.percentile() — understanding how to extract quartiles from a dataset
Interpreting IQR values in the context of data distribution and identifying potential outliers — recognizing when high IQR values indicate skewed distributions
Using IQR to normalize data for visualization purposes, such as scaling data to fit within a specific range — applying IQR to improve data visualization effectiveness
Calculating IQR for multiple datasets or variables using groupby() in Pandas and apply() functions — efficiently processing large datasets with IQR calculations
Visualizing IQR values using box plots or histograms in Tableau, Power BI, or Excel — effectively communicating IQR results to stakeholders

knowledge.what_it_is: Distribution refers to the shape and spread of data, describing how values are dispersed across the range of possible outcomes. In Business Intelligence (BI), understanding distribution is crucial for making informed decisions about data analysis, visualization, and modeling.
what_you_will_learn: Mean — the average value in a dataset, calculated using the formula x̄ = ∑x_i / n
Median — the middle value of a sorted dataset, used to describe central tendency when data is skewed or contains outliers
Mode — the most frequently occurring value in a dataset, often used for categorical data
Standard Deviation (σ) — a measure of dispersion that describes how much individual data points deviate from the mean
Variance (σ^2) — the average squared difference between each data point and the mean, calculated as σ^2 = ∑(x_i - x̄)^2 / n
Skewness — a measure of asymmetry in a distribution, indicating whether it's positively or negatively skewed
Kurtosis — a measure of the 'tailed-ness' of a distribution, describing how fat or thin its tails are compared to a normal distribution
Interquartile Range (IQR) — the difference between the 75th and 25th percentiles, used to describe dispersion in datasets with outliers
Box Plot — a graphical representation of a dataset's five-number summary: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum
Quantiles — dividing a dataset into equal-sized groups based on rank or value, often used for data visualization and analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: Skewness measures the asymmetry of a distribution, indicating whether data points are concentrated on one side or the other of the mean. It's essential for BI analysts to understand skewness when analyzing and interpreting data, as it can impact statistical analysis and modeling.
what_you_will_learn: Types of Skewness — Understanding the three types: positive (right-skewed), negative (left-skewed), and symmetric
Measures of Skewness — Learning to calculate skewness using different methods: Pearson's coefficient, Bowley's coefficient, and moment-based measures
Interpretation of Skewness Results — Understanding how to interpret skewness values and their implications for data analysis and modeling
Skewness in Different Distributions — Recognizing how skewness behaves in various distributions, such as normal, exponential, and logistic
Impact on Statistical Analysis — Understanding how skewness affects statistical tests and models, including regression and hypothesis testing
Handling Skewed Data — Learning techniques to address skewed data, including transformation methods (logarithmic, square root) and winsorization
Visualizing Skewness — Using plots and charts to visualize skewness, such as histograms, box plots, and Q-Q plots

knowledge.what_it_is: Kurtosis measures the tail heaviness of a distribution, indicating how extreme its values are compared to the normal distribution. It's an essential concept in data analysis and visualization, particularly when working with skewed or heavy-tailed distributions.
what_you_will_learn: Normality test — using statistical tests (e.g., Shapiro-Wilk, Anderson-Darling) to determine if a dataset is normally distributed
Kurtosis calculation — understanding the formula for calculating kurtosis in Excel (KURT function), Python (scipy.stats.kurtosis), or R (kurtosis() function)
Skewness vs. Kurtosis — distinguishing between skewness and kurtosis, including their implications on data analysis and visualization
Interpretation of Kurtosis values — understanding the meaning of different kurtosis values (e.g., platykurtic, leptokurtic, mesokurtic)
Kurtosis in Excel — using Excel's built-in functions (e.g., KURT, SKEW) to calculate and visualize kurtosis
Python libraries for Kurtosis calculation — familiarizing with popular Python libraries (e.g., pandas, scipy, statsmodels) for calculating kurtosis and related metrics
Kurtosis in data visualization — understanding how to effectively display and interpret kurtosis values in plots and charts

knowledge.what_it_is: Inferential statistics is a branch of statistics that deals with making conclusions or predictions about a population based on a sample of data, commonly used by Business Intelligence (BI) analysts to inform business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Hypothesis testing — the process of testing a hypothesis about a population parameter using sample data and statistical tests
Confidence intervals — constructing an interval estimate for a population parameter based on sample data, used to quantify uncertainty in estimates
Regression analysis — modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables using linear regression techniques
Anova (Analysis of Variance) — testing whether there are significant differences between means of multiple groups, often used in A/B testing and experimentation
Non-parametric tests — statistical tests that do not assume a normal distribution for the data, such as Wilcoxon rank-sum test or Kruskal-Wallis H-test
T-tests — comparing the means of two groups to determine if there is a significant difference between them
Chi-squared tests — testing whether there are significant differences in categorical variables between groups
Resampling methods (e.g. bootstrapping, permutation test) — techniques for estimating variability and uncertainty in estimates using resampled data
Bayesian inference — using Bayes' theorem to update prior probabilities based on new evidence from sample data
Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods — computational algorithms for sampling from complex probability distributions
Gaussian process regression — a non-parametric Bayesian approach to modeling relationships between variables

knowledge.what_it_is: Correlation analysis is a statistical technique used to measure and visualize the relationship between two or more variables in a dataset, helping BI analysts identify patterns, trends, and correlations that can inform business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Pearson correlation coefficient — measures linear correlation between two continuous variables, ranging from -1 (perfect negative correlation) to 1 (perfect positive correlation)
Spearman rank correlation coefficient — measures non-parametric correlation between two ranked variables, useful for ordinal data
Kendall's tau — measures non-parametric correlation between two ranked variables, similar to Spearman but with a different formula
Correlation matrix — visualizes the correlation between multiple pairs of variables, helping identify relationships and outliers
Heatmap — visualizes correlation matrix as a colored grid, highlighting strong correlations and clusters
Partial correlation — measures correlation between two variables while controlling for one or more other variables
Multiple linear regression — extends simple linear regression to include multiple independent variables and their interactions
subtopics: partial correlation vs multiple linear regression

knowledge.what_it_is: Correlation vs causation is a fundamental concept in business intelligence (BI) analysis, where correlation refers to the statistical relationship between two variables, while causation implies that one variable directly affects the other. As a BI analyst, understanding this distinction is crucial for avoiding false conclusions and making informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Pearson's r vs Spearman's rho — choosing the correct correlation coefficient for continuous vs ordinal data
Correlation matrix — visualizing relationships between multiple variables using heatmaps or scatterplots
Partial dependence plots — understanding how one variable affects another while controlling for other factors
Feature importance — calculating and interpreting the contribution of each feature to a model's predictions
Covariance vs correlation — distinguishing between linear and non-linear relationships in data
Confounding variables — identifying and adjusting for external factors that affect the relationship between two variables

knowledge.what_it_is: Linear regression is a widely used statistical method for modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables, commonly used in business intelligence (BI) to identify trends, forecast values, and understand relationships between variables.
what_you_will_learn: Simple vs Multiple Linear Regression — understanding the difference between single-variable and multi-variable regression models
Coefficient of Determination (R-squared) — calculating and interpreting the goodness-of-fit measure for linear regression models
Coefficient of Variation (CV) — understanding the ratio of standard deviation to mean, useful in comparing variability across different datasets
Residual Analysis — identifying and addressing issues with residuals, such as non-normality or heteroscedasticity
Feature Scaling — applying techniques like Standardization or Normalization to ensure features have similar magnitudes for linear regression models
Regularization Techniques (L1 & L2) — understanding how to prevent overfitting by adding penalties to model complexity
Linear Regression with Categorical Variables — handling categorical predictors using one-hot encoding, label encoding, or dummy variables

knowledge.what_it_is: Linear regression is a fundamental technique for modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables, but it has its limitations. Beyond linear regression, BI analysts use various techniques to model complex relationships and improve predictive accuracy.
what_you_will_learn: Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) — extending linear regression to handle non-normal response distributions using link functions
Poisson Regression — modeling count data with a Poisson distribution and log-link function
Logistic Regression — binary classification using logistic link function and maximum likelihood estimation
Decision Trees and Random Forests — ensemble methods for handling non-linear relationships and interactions between variables
Support Vector Machines (SVM) — kernel-based methods for high-dimensional data and non-linear relationships
Gradient Boosting — sequential combination of weak models to improve predictive accuracy
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) — instance-based learning for handling complex, non-linear relationships
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Partial Least Squares (PLS) — dimensionality reduction techniques for high-dimensional data

knowledge.what_it_is: Hypothesis testing is a statistical method used to test a hypothesis or assumption about a population based on a sample of data, commonly used in Business Intelligence (BI) analysis to validate business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Null Hypothesis — the default assumption that there is no significant difference between groups or populations
Alternative Hypothesis — the hypothesis that there is a significant difference between groups or populations
P-Value — the probability of observing the test statistic under the null hypothesis, used to determine significance
Significance Level (α) — the maximum probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, typically set at 0.05
Type I Error — the error of rejecting a true null hypothesis, also known as alpha error
Type II Error — the error of failing to reject a false null hypothesis, also known as beta error
Confidence Interval (CI) — a range of values within which we expect the population parameter to lie with a certain level of confidence
Hypothesis Testing Frameworks — including ANOVA, T-Test, Non-Parametric Tests, and Regression Analysis
Independence Assumption — the assumption that observations are independent of each other in a dataset
Normality Assumption — the assumption that data follows a normal distribution, often checked using statistical tests like Shapiro-Wilk or Q-Q plots
Homogeneity of Variance (HOV) Assumption — the assumption that variances are equal across groups or populations
Levene's Test and Bartlett's Test — statistical tests used to check HOV
Kruskal-Wallis H-Test and Mann-Whitney U-Test — non-parametric alternatives for ANOVA and T-Test respectively
Multiple Testing Correction Methods — including Bonferroni, Holm-Bonferroni, and Benjamini-Hochberg procedures to account for multiple comparisons

knowledge.what_it_is: Statistical tests are used to determine whether observed differences between groups are statistically significant, often used in Business Intelligence (BI) analysis to validate hypotheses and make informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Hypothesis testing — the process of testing a hypothesis about a population parameter based on sample data
Null and alternative hypotheses — defining the null hypothesis as a statement of no effect or no difference, and the alternative hypothesis as a statement of an effect or difference
P-value — the probability of observing the test statistic under the null hypothesis, used to determine statistical significance
Type I and Type II errors — understanding the risks of rejecting a true null hypothesis (Type I error) and failing to reject a false null hypothesis (Type II error)
Confidence intervals — constructing intervals around sample statistics to estimate population parameters with a specified level of confidence
T-tests and ANOVA — using these statistical tests to compare means between groups, including one-sample, two-sample, and multiple-group comparisons
Non-parametric tests — applying tests such as the Wilcoxon rank-sum test and Kruskal-Wallis H-test when data does not meet parametric assumptions
Regression analysis with statistical tests — using statistical tests to evaluate the significance of regression coefficients in linear models

knowledge.what_it_is: The p-value, or probability value, is a statistical measure used to determine whether an observed effect is due to chance or is statistically significant. In the context of Business Intelligence (BI) analysis, p-values are crucial for hypothesis testing and decision-making.
what_you_will_learn: Null Hypothesis — understanding the concept of a null hypothesis in statistical testing, including its role in hypothesis testing
Alternative Hypothesis — learning about alternative hypotheses, including one-tailed and two-tailed tests
p-value Calculation — understanding how to calculate p-values using various statistical methods, such as t-tests and ANOVA
Interpretation of p-value — learning how to interpret the results of a p-value calculation, including determining statistical significance
Type I Error vs Type II Error — understanding the difference between Type I errors (false positives) and Type II errors (false negatives)
p-value Thresholds — knowing the common thresholds for p-values, such as 0.05 and 0.01, and their implications for decision-making

knowledge.what_it_is: A confidence interval is a statistical tool used to estimate a population parameter, such as a mean or proportion, based on a sample of data. It provides a range of values within which the true population parameter is likely to lie with a certain level of confidence.
what_you_will_learn: Z-scores — calculating critical values for large samples using the standard normal distribution
T-scores — calculating critical values for small samples using the t-distribution
Margin of error — understanding the trade-off between sample size and margin of error in estimating population parameters
Confidence level — selecting an appropriate confidence level (e.g. 95%) based on business requirements and risk tolerance
Sample size determination — calculating required sample sizes for different types of studies using formulas like Cochran's formula
Interval estimation — constructing confidence intervals for means, proportions, and other parameters using various methods (e.g. Wilson score interval)
Bootstrap confidence intervals — using resampling techniques to estimate population parameters with improved accuracy
Confidence interval interpretation — understanding how to communicate the results of a study to stakeholders, including limitations and potential biases

knowledge.what_it_is: In hypothesis testing, types of errors refer to mistakes that can occur when making conclusions based on sample data. Type I error occurs when rejecting a true null hypothesis, while type II error occurs when failing to reject a false null hypothesis.
what_you_will_learn: Type I Error — the probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis, often denoted as α (alpha) and typically set at 0.05
Type II Error — the probability of failing to reject a false null hypothesis, often denoted as β (beta)
Power — the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis, calculated as 1 - β
Margin of Error — the maximum difference between the sample statistic and the population parameter, used to construct confidence intervals
Confidence Interval Width — the range within which the true population parameter is likely to lie, influenced by the margin of error and sample size
P-Value Threshold — the critical value for determining significance, often set at 0.05 but can vary depending on the context
Significance Level (α) vs Power Tradeoff — understanding how adjusting α affects power and vice versa
Type III Error — a rare error that occurs when the null hypothesis is true but the alternative hypothesis is also false

knowledge.what_it_is: In Business Intelligence (BI) analysis, population and sample refer to the entire set of data points (population) and a subset of that data used for analysis (sample). A well-defined population is essential for accurate sampling.
what_you_will_learn: Population — the complete set of data points, including all relevant variables and characteristics
Sample — a representative subset of the population, used to make inferences about the larger dataset
Sampling methods — techniques for selecting samples from the population, such as random sampling, stratified sampling, or systematic sampling
Sampling frame — the list of items from which the sample is drawn, ensuring it's representative of the population
Sample size determination — calculating the optimal number of observations to include in the sample based on desired precision and confidence levels
Bias and variability — understanding how sampling methods can introduce bias or increase variability in the results
Confidence intervals — constructing intervals around estimates to quantify uncertainty and make informed decisions

knowledge.what_it_is: A database is a collection of organized data that can be accessed, managed, and updated efficiently. In the context of Business Intelligence (BI), databases are used to store and manage large datasets, providing insights for informed decision-making.
what_you_will_learn: Relational databases — structured data storage using tables, rows, and columns, with SQL as the primary query language
NoSQL databases — flexible schema-less data storage using key-value, document, or graph models, with MongoDB, Cassandra, and Couchbase as popular examples
Data modeling — designing database structures to optimize data relationships, performance, and scalability
Database normalization — organizing data into tables to minimize data redundancy and improve data integrity
Denormalization — storing redundant data in a single table for improved query performance
Indexing and caching — optimizing database queries using indexes, materialized views, and caching mechanisms
Data warehousing — designing centralized repositories for business analytics, using star or snowflake schema designs
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) tools — automating data integration from various sources into a single database

knowledge.what_it_is: The web refers to the collection of websites, online services, and applications that use HTTP/HTTPS protocols to communicate between clients (browsers) and servers. As a BI Analyst, you will work with web data from various sources, including databases, APIs, and file systems.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP methods — understanding GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD, OPTIONS for web interactions
Web scraping — using libraries like Beautiful Soup or Scrapy to extract data from websites
API design principles — designing RESTful APIs with endpoints, query parameters, and authentication mechanisms
JSON data format — working with JSON objects, arrays, and nested structures in web applications
Web storage options — understanding cookies, local storage, session storage, and IndexedDB for client-side data management
Web security best practices — implementing HTTPS, input validation, and CSRF protection to prevent web attacks

knowledge.what_it_is: Analog data refers to information collected from physical sources, such as sensors, surveys, or manual entry, whereas digital data is stored electronically and can be easily processed and analyzed. As a BI Analyst, understanding the difference between analog and digital data is crucial for making informed business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Analog vs Digital Data — key differences in collection, storage, and analysis
Data Sources — identifying analog sources (e.g., sensors, surveys) and digital sources (e.g., databases, APIs)
Data Types — understanding the characteristics of analog data (e.g., categorical, numerical) and digital data (e.g., structured, unstructured)
Data Quality Issues — common problems with analog data (e.g., measurement errors, missing values) and digital data (e.g., data breaches, format inconsistencies)
Digital Data Formats — understanding file formats for digital data (e.g., CSV, JSON, XML) and their implications for analysis
Analog to Digital Conversion — techniques for converting analog data into digital form (e.g., scanning, OCR)
Data Integration — combining analog and digital data sources for comprehensive analysis
subtopics: data types in analog vs digital data
data quality metrics for analog data
data quality metrics for digital data
file formats for digital data (csv, json, xml)
data preprocessing techniques for analog data
data preprocessing techniques for digital data
data visualization best practices for analog data
data visualization best practices for digital data

knowledge.what_it_is: Structured data refers to organized and formatted data that follows a predefined schema, making it easily accessible and queryable by BI tools and software. In the context of Business Intelligence (BI), structured data is typically stored in relational databases such as MySQL or PostgreSQL.
what_you_will_learn: Relational database management systems — understanding the concept of tables, rows, and columns, and how they are used to store and retrieve data
SQL syntax — learning basic SQL queries such as SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, and JOIN to extract and manipulate structured data
Data modeling techniques — understanding entity-relationship diagrams (ERDs) and how to design a logical data model for a BI system
Normalization rules — applying normalization techniques such as 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF to ensure data consistency and minimize data redundancy
Data types and datatypes — understanding the difference between numeric, character, date/time, and boolean data types in structured databases

Indexing and partitioning strategies — learning how to optimize query performance by creating indexes on columns used in WHERE clauses and partitioning large tables for efficient storage and retrieval

knowledge.what_it_is: Semistructured data refers to data that has some structure, but also contains free-form or unstructured elements, such as text fields or images. In BI analysis, semistructured data is often encountered in CSV files, Excel spreadsheets, or JSON documents.
what_you_will_learn: XML Schema — defining the structure of semistructured data using XML schema language
JSON Schema — validating and documenting JSON-based semistructured data
CSV parsing libraries (e.g. pandas, csvkit) — efficiently reading and processing large CSV files
Data cleansing techniques for semistructured data (e.g. handling missing values, data normalization)
Regular expressions for text pattern matching in semistructured data
Image processing libraries (e.g. Pillow, OpenCV) — extracting metadata from images
Semistructured data modeling using entity-relationship diagrams or data warehousing techniques

knowledge.what_it_is: Unstructured data refers to information that does not conform to a predefined format or schema, such as text documents, images, audio files, and videos. In the context of Business Intelligence (BI), unstructured data is often used for exploratory analysis, sentiment analysis, and topic modeling.
what_you_will_learn: Text Preprocessing — techniques for cleaning and normalizing text data, including tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, and removing stop words
Sentiment Analysis — methods for determining the emotional tone of text, such as using machine learning algorithms or rule-based approaches like VADER or NLTK

Topic Modeling — statistical techniques for identifying underlying themes or topics in large collections of text data, including Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and Non-Negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)
Image Processing — methods for analyzing and extracting insights from images, such as using OpenCV for feature extraction or deep learning-based approaches like convolutional neural networks (CNNs)
Audio Analysis — techniques for processing and understanding audio data, including speech recognition, music classification, and environmental sound analysis
Video Analysis — methods for examining video content, including object detection, tracking, and sentiment analysis using tools like OpenCV or deep learning libraries like TensorFlow or PyTorch
subtopics: text mining tools like nltk or spacy
deep learning architectures for image and video analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: Types of data refer to the categorization of data based on its structure, organization, and format, which is crucial for Business Intelligence (BI) analysts to understand when designing and implementing data visualizations, reports, and analytics.
what_you_will_learn: Structured data — organized into rows and columns in a table format, typically stored in relational databases like MySQL or PostgreSQL, with examples including customer information, sales transactions, and financial records

Semistructured data — has some level of organization but is not strictly structured, often stored in NoSQL databases like MongoDB or Cassandra, with examples including social media posts, emails, and sensor readings

Unstructured data — lacks any inherent structure or organization, typically stored in file systems or cloud storage services like AWS S3, with examples including images, videos, audio files, and text documents

Quantitative data — numerical values that can be measured or counted, often used for statistical analysis and forecasting, with examples including sales revenue, website traffic, and customer demographics

Qualitative data — non-numerical values that provide context and insight, often used for market research and customer feedback, with examples including survey responses, social media sentiment, and customer reviews
Time-series data — numerical values measured at regular time intervals, often used for forecasting and trend analysis, with examples including stock prices, website traffic, and weather data
Geospatial data — related to geographic locations, often used for mapping and spatial analysis, with examples including GPS coordinates, addresses, and boundary polygons

Categorical data — labels or categories that describe a characteristic, often used for segmentation and clustering analysis, with examples including customer segments, product categories, and demographic groups

knowledge.what_it_is: Microsoft Excel is a spreadsheet software used for data analysis, visualization, and manipulation. As a BI Analyst, you will use Excel to extract insights from large datasets, create reports, and perform data modeling.
what_you_will_learn: Formulas — creating calculations using cell references, functions, and operators (e.g., SUM, AVERAGE, IF)
PivotTables — summarizing and analyzing large datasets by aggregating values and creating dynamic reports
VLOOKUP and INDEX/MATCH — performing lookups and matching data between tables
Conditional Formatting — applying visual styles to cells based on conditions or formulas (e.g., highlighting errors or trends)
Data Validation — restricting user input to specific formats, ranges, or lists
Macros — automating repetitive tasks using VBA code and recording macros in the Visual Basic Editor
Power Query — importing, transforming, and combining data from various sources into a single dataset
Power Pivot — creating data models for advanced analysis, including data relationships and measures

knowledge.what_it_is: Mobile apps are software applications designed to run on mobile devices, such as smartphones and tablets. As a BI Analyst, you will work with mobile app data to provide insights and recommendations to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Native vs Hybrid Apps — understanding the differences between native iOS and Android apps, and hybrid frameworks like React Native and Flutter
Mobile App Development Frameworks — learning about popular frameworks such as React Native, Flutter, Xamarin, and Ionic for building cross-platform mobile apps
Data Storage in Mobile Apps — understanding how to store data locally on mobile devices using SQLite, Realm, or other databases
API Integration with Mobile Apps — learning how to integrate APIs from cloud services like AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure into mobile apps
Mobile App Analytics Tools — familiarizing yourself with tools like Firebase Analytics, Google Analytics, and Mixpanel for tracking user behavior and app performance
Security in Mobile Apps — understanding the importance of encryption, secure data storage, and authentication mechanisms to protect sensitive user data
Mobile App Testing and Debugging — learning about testing frameworks like Appium, Espresso, and JUnit, as well as debugging tools like Android Studio and Xcode

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud computing enables on-demand access to scalable and flexible IT resources, such as servers, storage, and applications, over the internet. As a BI Analyst, you'll work with cloud-based data platforms like Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, or Microsoft Azure Synapse Analytics to analyze large datasets.
what_you_will_learn: Cloud Service Models — understanding IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service), PaaS (Platform as a Service), and SaaS (Software as a Service) for BI workloads
Cloud Deployment Models — learning about Public Clouds, Private Clouds, Hybrid Clouds, and Community Clouds for data analysis
Amazon Web Services (AWS) — familiarizing yourself with AWS services like Amazon Redshift, Amazon S3, Amazon DynamoDB, and AWS Lake Formation for big data analytics
Google Cloud Platform (GCP) — understanding GCP services like Google BigQuery, Google Cloud Storage, Google Cloud Dataflow, and Google Cloud Dataproc for data processing and analysis
Microsoft Azure — learning about Azure services like Azure Synapse Analytics, Azure Data Factory, Azure Databricks, and Azure Machine Learning for enterprise-scale analytics
Cloud Security and Governance — understanding best practices for securing cloud-based data platforms, including identity and access management, encryption, and auditing
Data Ingestion and ETL — learning about tools like AWS Glue, GCP Cloud Dataflow, or Azure Data Factory for extracting, transforming, and loading data into cloud-based data warehouses

knowledge.what_it_is: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are software intermediaries that enable different systems, services, or applications to communicate with each other, exchanging data and functionality. In the context of Business Intelligence (BI), APIs play a crucial role in integrating various data sources, processing large datasets, and providing insights to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: API Design Patterns — understanding RESTful API design principles, including resource-based URI structures, HTTP methods, and status codes
API Security — implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms, such as OAuth, JWT, and role-based access control
API Gateway — using tools like AWS API Gateway, Google Cloud Endpoints, or Azure API Management to manage API traffic, security, and analytics
API Documentation — creating clear and concise documentation for APIs using tools like Swagger, API Blueprint, or Redoc
API Testing — writing unit tests and integration tests for APIs using frameworks like Pytest, Unittest, or Jest
API Monitoring — setting up monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic to track API performance and latency
API Integration — integrating APIs with ETL tools like Informatica PowerCenter, Talend, or Microsoft SSIS
API Data Modeling — designing data models for APIs using entities, attributes, and relationships

knowledge.what_it_is: IoT (Internet of Things) refers to the network of physical devices, vehicles, home appliances, and other items embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity, enabling them to collect and exchange data. As a BI Analyst, you'll work with IoT data from various sources, such as industrial equipment, smart homes, or wearables.
what_you_will_learn: IoT Data Sources — understanding the different types of IoT devices and their data formats (e.g., JSON, CSV), including popular protocols like MQTT and CoAP
Device Management — learning about device registration, authentication, and authorization in IoT ecosystems, using tools like AWS IoT Core or Google Cloud IoT Core
Data Ingestion — understanding how to collect and process IoT data from various sources, including APIs, file uploads, and message queues (e.g., Apache Kafka)
Sensor Data Analysis — learning about common sensor types (e.g., temperature, humidity, motion), their data processing requirements, and visualization techniques
Edge Computing — understanding the role of edge devices in IoT, including processing, storage, and analytics at the device level, using frameworks like EdgeX or AWS Greengrass
IoT Data Visualization — learning about best practices for visualizing IoT data, including dashboarding tools like Tableau or Power BI, and interactive visualizations with D3.js or Plotly
Security and Governance — understanding IoT security threats (e.g., device compromise, data breaches) and governance frameworks (e.g., NIST Cybersecurity Framework)
IoT Data Quality — learning about common issues in IoT data (e.g., missing values, outliers), and techniques for data cleaning and preprocessing

knowledge.what_it_is: Comma Separated Values (CSV) is a widely used file format for storing and exchanging tabular data, commonly used in Business Intelligence (BI) applications to import/export data from various sources.
what_you_will_learn: CSV structure — understanding the basic syntax of CSV files, including header rows, delimiter usage, and quoting rules
Data types in CSV — learning how to handle different data types such as integers, strings, dates, and numbers in CSV files
CSV parsing libraries — familiarizing yourself with popular libraries like pandas (Python), csvkit (command-line tool), or csv-parser (JavaScript) for efficient CSV processing
Handling missing values — understanding how to deal with missing data in CSV files using techniques such as imputation, interpolation, or deletion
CSV vs other formats — comparing the advantages and disadvantages of CSV format with other popular formats like JSON, XML, or Excel files
Data quality checks — learning how to perform basic data quality checks on CSV files, including syntax validation, data type consistency, and value range checking

knowledge.what_it_is: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data interchange format used to exchange data between web servers, web applications, and mobile apps. It is widely adopted in BI and data analysis due to its simplicity and flexibility.
what_you_will_learn: Data types — JSON supports five basic data types: strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, and objects
JSON syntax — understanding the rules for writing valid JSON, including indentation, escaping special characters, and using quotes around property names
Arrays in JSON — representing collections of values as ordered lists, with optional indexing and slicing
Objects in JSON — representing complex data structures as key-value pairs, with support for nested objects and arrays
JSON parsing and serialization — understanding how to convert between JSON strings and native data types using libraries like json.loads() and json.dumps()
JSON schema validation — using tools like jsonschema to define and enforce the structure of JSON data
Handling missing values in JSON — strategies for dealing with null or undefined values, including defaulting and imputation

knowledge.what_it_is: XML (Extensible Markup Language) is a markup language used to store and transport data between systems, independent of platform or vendor. It's widely used in Business Intelligence (BI) for data exchange, integration, and reporting.
what_you_will_learn: XML Schema — defining the structure and constraints of an XML document using XSD (XML Schema Definition)
XSLT (XSL Transformations) — transforming XML documents into other formats like HTML or CSV
XPath (XML Path Language) — selecting nodes in an XML document using a query language
XQuery — querying and manipulating XML data using a declarative language
XML Parsing — reading and processing XML files using libraries like SAX, DOM, or StAX
XML Validation — checking the structure and content of an XML document against a schema or DTD (Document Type Definition)
XML Encoding — representing special characters in XML documents using entities or character references

knowledge.what_it_is: Other data formats used in Business Intelligence, including Avro, Parquet, and ORC, which are optimized for big data processing and provide efficient storage and querying capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Avro — a row-based format that stores schema along with the data, enabling flexible and efficient data exchange between systems
Parquet — a columnar storage format that enables fast query performance and supports complex data types like arrays and maps
ORC (Optimized Row Columnar) — a columnar storage format that provides high compression ratios and efficient querying capabilities for large datasets
Protobuf (Protocol Buffers) — a language-agnostic data serialization format used in many big data systems, including Apache Beam and Apache Flink
MessagePack — a binary-based data serialization format that is compact and fast, often used in real-time analytics and IoT applications

knowledge.what_it_is: Basic queries are fundamental SQL statements used to retrieve and manipulate data from relational databases, essential skills for BI Analysts to extract insights from large datasets.
what_you_will_learn: SELECT statement — retrieving specific columns and rows from a table
WHERE clause — filtering data based on conditions using logical operators (AND, OR, NOT)
FROM clause — specifying the tables involved in the query
JOINs — combining data from multiple tables based on common columns (INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN)
GROUP BY clause — aggregating data by one or more columns (SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN, COUNT)
HAVING clause — filtering grouped results based on conditions
ORDER BY clause — sorting query results in ascending or descending order
LIMIT clause — restricting the number of rows returned

knowledge.what_it_is: Advanced queries are complex SQL statements that use various techniques to optimize performance, handle large datasets, and extract meaningful insights from data. In the context of a BI Analyst role, advanced queries are essential for creating efficient and effective reports, dashboards, and data visualizations.
what_you_will_learn: Subqueries — using nested SQL statements to perform complex calculations, filtering, or aggregation
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — defining temporary result sets that can be referenced within a query
Window Functions with PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clauses — performing calculations across rows within a partition of a result set
Ranking Functions (RANK, DENSE_RANK, PERCENT_RANK) — assigning ranks to rows based on a specific column or expression
Offset and Fetch — efficiently retrieving large datasets by skipping unnecessary records and limiting the number of returned rows
EXISTS vs IN — choosing the optimal method for checking existence in a subquery
JOINs with multiple tables — combining data from multiple sources using various join types (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER)
UNION ALL vs UNION DISTINCT — merging result sets while preserving or eliminating duplicate rows
Using INDEX hints and covering indexes to optimize query performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Window functions are a type of SQL function that allow you to perform calculations across rows that are related to the current row, often used in data analysis and reporting. They are particularly useful when working with time-series or hierarchical data.
what_you_will_learn: ROW_NUMBER() — assigns a unique number to each row within a partition of a result set
RANK() — ranks rows within a partition based on a specific column, handling ties with the same rank
DENSE_RANK() — similar to RANK(), but without gaps in ranking due to tied values
NTILE() — divides an ordered dataset into a specified number of groups (or buckets)
LAG() and LEAD() — access data from previous or next row(s) within a result set, respectively
FIRST_VALUE() and LAST_VALUE() — return the first or last value within a window frame for a specific column
PERCENT_RANK() — calculates the relative rank of each row within a partition as a percentage
CUME_DIST() — returns the proportion of rows that are below a given row in a sorted result set

knowledge.what_it_is: Performance optimization is crucial for BI analysts to ensure efficient data processing, querying, and reporting. It involves identifying bottlenecks, optimizing database queries, and leveraging caching mechanisms to improve system responsiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Query optimization techniques — rewriting SQL queries to reduce execution time, using indexes, and minimizing subqueries
Indexing strategies — creating and maintaining indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses
Caching mechanisms — implementing caching layers like Redis or Memcached to store frequently accessed data
Data partitioning — dividing large datasets into smaller, manageable chunks for improved query performance
Materialized views — precomputing complex queries and storing results in physical tables for faster access
